parsed.summary: The v0.4.x cumulative diff introduces retry logic, BigModel error dispatch, and job-tracking changes that have multiple material gaps: retry logic may silently swallow non-retriable errors or retry too aggressively on transient failures, state management has TOCTOU race potential on concurrent job mutations, and the glm-client error dispatch path has a fallback that could mask real failures. I need to inspect the actual code to confirm and ground these findings.

findings.title: retry.mjs may retry on non-idempotent operations or swallow non-retriable errors
findings.body: The new retry module (203 lines added) is a generic retry wrapper. If callers invoke it on operations with side effects (job state mutations, API calls that aren't idempotent), a retried request could cause duplicate actions or corrupted state. Without seeing explicit idempotency guards or retry eligibility classification in the caller chain (glm-client.mjs, tracked-jobs.mjs), this is a concrete risk for data duplication or double-submission to the BigModel API. The tests file (352 lines) may cover unit cases but likely doesn't cover concurrent retry + state mutation scenarios.
findings.recommendation: Audit every call site of the retry wrapper to confirm the operation is idempotent or guarded by an idempotency key. Add explicit retry-eligibility classification (retriable vs non-retriable error codes) and ensure non-retriable errors surface immediately without consuming retries.

findings.title: glm-client.mjs error dispatch has a catch-all that could mask real failures silently
findings.body: glm-client.mjs gained 155 lines of changes including BigModel error dispatch integration (bigmodel-errors.mjs). The risk is a catch-all error handler that classifies unknown errors as a generic fallback (e.g., returning null, empty result, or a default error) instead of propagating the failure. In a sampling sweep context, silent failures mean the sweep completes with partial/missing data and the user never knows. This is exactly the kind of observability gap the attack surface calls out.
findings.recommendation: Verify that the error dispatch in glm-client.mjs has no catch-all branch that swallows unknown error types. Every unrecognized error must propagate as a hard failure with enough context (status, body, endpoint) to diagnose. Add a test case for an unexpected error shape to confirm it isn't silently handled.

findings.title: state.mjs and tracked-jobs.mjs concurrent mutation risk (TOCTOU on job state)
findings.body: state.mjs (32 lines changed) and tracked-jobs.mjs (78 lines changed) manage job state that is read and written in separate operations. If two concurrent processes or overlapping retries both read-modify-write the same job state file, the last writer wins and state is lost. This is a classic TOCTOU race. In a sampling-sweep with parallel or overlapping runs, this could cause jobs to be lost, duplicated, or stuck in a permanently pending state with no recovery path.
findings.recommendation: Add atomic write semantics (write-to-temp + rename) if not already present, and consider file locking or optimistic concurrency with version checks. At minimum, document the concurrency model and confirm that sampling-sweep never runs overlapping mutations on the same state file.

findings.title: bigmodel-errors.mjs error classification may misclassify rate-limit vs auth failures
findings.body: bigmodel-errors.mjs is a new 168-line module for error dispatch. Incorrect classification of HTTP status codes (e.g., treating a 401/403 as retriable, or a 429 as non-retriable) would cause either infinite retry loops on auth errors or premature abandonment on rate limits. The tests (189 lines) likely cover happy-path classification but may not cover edge cases like 502/503/504 distinctions or non-standard BigModel error response shapes.
findings.recommendation: Verify that 401/403 are classified as non-retriable (fail immediately), 429 is retriable with backoff based on Retry-After header, and 5xx errors have bounded retries. Add explicit test cases for each HTTP status class to prevent regression.

findings.title: process.mjs deleted — verify no remaining imports or runtime references
findings.body: scripts/lib/process.mjs was deleted entirely (67 lines removed). If any other module still imports from it (dynamic import, string-based require, or indirect dependency), this will cause a runtime ReferenceError that only surfaces when that code path executes. The diff stat shows many files changed, increasing the chance of a stale import surviving.
findings.recommendation: Grep the entire codebase for any remaining references to 'process.mjs' or exports from it (scripts/check-imports.mjs may already do this — verify it covers dynamic imports and re-exports). Confirm no runtime path in glm-companion.mjs or any hook references the deleted module.

next_steps: Inspect scripts/lib/retry.mjs and scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs directly to confirm idempotency and error propagation behavior
Inspect scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs to verify HTTP status code classification is correct (401/403 non-retriable, 429 retriable)
Inspect scripts/lib/state.mjs and tracked-jobs.mjs for atomicity of read-modify-write on job state
Grep for any remaining references to the deleted process.mjs
Run the existing test suite to confirm no regressions from the process.mjs deletion
